course_title: Politics, Philosophy, Economics 4: Toward Social and Economic Reform

assessments.mode: Activity/ Performance, Oral, Product/ Artefact/ Multimedia, Written
assessments.task_description:
Individual participation scores will be awarded on the basis of participation during in-class activities. Attendance is a necessary but not sufficient condition for a participation grade. Attendance is mandatory and will be recorded each week. Students are assessed on their ability to contribute to a community of inquiry, through contributions to teamwork, peer feedback sessions, and in-class discussions. The ability to work collaboratively to progress arguments, generate critical questions, test ideas, and evaluate competing interpretations and points of view are highly valued skills for both future research and employment. 

Alternative or make-up work will be assigned to students who miss a seminar for medical or other legitimate reasons only. Documentation of the reason for the absence is required. Students with an accommodation must contact the course coordinator to arrange reasonable adjustments before the date of the activity.

 

Components:

Seminar Participation (10%)

During seminars, students are expected to:

  1. Demonstrate evidence of comprehension of the texts assigned for reading each week and preparation of critical questions. Required readings will be marked 'required' and made available through Talis Aspire.
  2. Complete set tasks during class.
  3. Upload artefacts from classwork to relevant Turnitin assignments as required.


Seminar artefacts for uploading to Turnitin assignments:

  1. Team Charter and Peer and Self Evaluation forms (week 1)
  2. Practice argument map (week 2)
  3. Systems map (week 5)
  4. Scenario matrix (week 6)
  5. Value proposition canvas (week 7)
  6. Story telling artefact (week 9)
  7. Position statement and policy brief executive summary (week 11)


Criteria & Marking: Participation

 Your participation will be assessed according to the following criteria:

1.             Evidence of participation in discussion and group activities.

2.             Evidence of preparation for class, mostly through completing the set readings and/or independent research related to your inequality challenge and preparation of questions and comments related to the reading.

3.             Quality of contributions to discussions.

4.             Fostering of a community of inquiry, through courteous and respectful interactions with staff and students. A Community of Inquiry involves students working to progress ideas or arguments and solve problems together.

Note on the use of AI:

This assessment is designed to protect academic integrity and assess your understanding of course concepts. You must only use the approved materials and tools for this assessment task. Use of prohibited materials in this task may be investigated and treated as academic misconduct. See assessment instructions for more details.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This is work conducted and assessed during class. Submit artefacts to relevant Turnitin assignment. Photos of whiteboard work or drawings is fine.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Individual in-class assessment: Construct a complex argument map integrating philosophical, political, and economic arguments for or against the conclusion that we* should act to minimise social and economic inequalities. Include counterarguments and rebuttals. (600 words)

(*'We' might refer to the Australian government adopting certain policies or we the people -- we collectively as members of society in Australia. The context either way is Australia.)

Use the reasons.io software to construct your map, export it to a Word document, append any bibliography you have prepared in advance, and submit to Turnitin by the allotted finishing time in the class. Use in-text referencing to identify sources of ideas where appropriate. See Blackbaord site for more details about argument mapping and this task.

Assessment Criteria

Students will be assessed on their:

  1. Ability to identify, analyse, and synthesise arguments in texts.
  2. Ability to evaluate arguments for validity and soundness.
  3. Ability to construct arguments and counterarguments.
  4. Insight and/or originality in constructing arguments
  5. Capacity to produce a comprehensible and concise argument map.


Note on the use of AI:

This assessment is designed to protect academic integrity and assess your understanding of course concepts. You must only use the approved materials and tools for this assessment task. Use of prohibited materials in this task may be investigated and treated as academic misconduct. See assessment instructions for more details.

Academic Misconduct: Referencing

Fabrication or misrepresentation of sources is considered a breach of academic integrity and may be referred for academic misconduct, regardless of performance in other criteria. This includes but is not limited to: citing non-existent sources, attributing false quotes to real sources, or not including accurate page numbers.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit to 'Argument Map' Turnitin assignment by the alloted end-time in class. Upload map as a readable image. Include reasons.io url.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.mode: Activity/ Performance, Oral, Product/ Artefact/ Multimedia, Written
assessments.task_description:
Policy-making in Australia has historically suffered from 'short-terminism'--strategic thinking and planning bounded within 3 to 4 year election cycles. For wicked problems like entrenched social and economic inequalities, longer-term strategic thinking is required. Imagine you are working for a government office that seeks to break the cycle of short-termism by engaging its staff in more futures thinking. Your target is 2045. What could we as a nation plausibly achieve between now and then that might help to address the problems associated with your specific social and economic inequality? In teams of 4, you are required to engage in a scenario planning strategic thinking exercise, where you are asked to:

  1. Identify the driving forces that shape the environment in which your inequality is manifested
  2. Identify 2 critical uncertainties and construct a 2x2 matrix
  3. Populate the quadrants with plausible scenarios (individual students take responsibility for 1 quadrant each)
  4. Generate a group strategy to lead Australia to a preferred future among the possible/ plausible ones identified.


This is a four-part task combining individual and group assessment tasks.

Part A: Preparation and Planning (Group; hurdle requirement)

Students will form themselves or be assigned into groups of four. The first task is to settle which inequality you specifically want to work on. (Examples include income, wealth, health, educational, housing, racial, ethnic, gender, geographical, age, disability, etc.) Design a Team Charter and a Peer and Self Evaluation form to to ensure equal contributions, effective communication, and respectful disagreement resolution processes.

Draw on megatrends and other data sources to identify the driving forces of the inequality. Identify 2 critical uncertainties, construct the matrix, and assign quadrants to team members. (4 members per team is the norm but if, due to class numbers, there are 5 team members in a particular group, 2 individuals may work on the same quadrant but the scenarios produced must be substantially different from each other.) Teams should meet regularly outside of class time to compare and synthesise results, provide peer feedback to strengthen each quadrant, and begin formulating a group strategy.

This is a team assignment as befitting futures thinking and strategic planning in organisational contexts, but teams will be required to delegate tasks to individual members and document individual contributions throughout the scenario planning process.


Part B: Scenario Planning Report (25%) (Individual scenario + artefact from the future = 2500 words (25%))

Scenario: Students produce individual scenarios, with feedback from peers and mentors. Students must demonstrate why each scenario is a plausible future based on current trends, signals, and imaginative projections. Scenarios must show evidence of integrating philosophical, political, and economic perspectives as well as concepts and principles from the strategic thinking skillset (systems, strategic, entrepreneurial, and futures thinking). The group strategy should make clear how the strategy could improve equitable outcomes in any of the four scenarios and how it might enable Australia to move toward a preferred future among those captured in the matrix.

Artefact: Choose one kind of artefact from the future (e.g., media release; day-in-the-life vignette; etc) and write a narrative based on the scenario to surface potential and significant secondary consequences. Include this as an inserted text-box in your scenario plan.


Part C: Group strategy (5%; 500 words) and Presentation (10%)

Student teams will present to the class, including lecturing staff, on the progress of their scenario planning exercise and group strategy in week 10 and receive feedback from the class. Groups have 10 minutes to present. First speaker nominates to introduce the topic and then presents their scenario (~3 minutes), followed by 2 other members of the group (~2 mins). The final speaker presents their scenario and the group strategy (~3 minutes).

The order of team presentations will be randomly decided. Other students are tasked with playing Devil's Advocate, stress testing strategies presented to help improve their robustness before submission.


Part D: Peer and self evaluation form (300 words; hurdle requirement)

Each member of the team will be required to submit a Peer and Self Evalation providing an honest reflection of their contributions and those of their team to the scenario planning process. The evaluation form should reflect the commitments agreed to originally in the Team Charter.


Consult the Scenario Planning Task Sheet under Blackboard for more detail and a sample contributor statement and peer and self evaluation template.


Note on AI:

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies to assist with research, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will at best provide only limited support and guidance. Students must declare any use of AI on the coversheet to be attached to the front of their individual scenario submission.

In this assessment you can use resources such as your notes, course materials, and other tools, including AI, while completing the task. The focus is not on memorising information, but on understanding, applying judgement, and demonstrating your own thinking.

Academic Misconduct: Referencing

Fabrication or misrepresentation of sources is considered a breach of academic integrity and may be referred for academic misconduct, regardless of performance in other criteria. This includes but is not limited to: citing non-existent sources, attributing false quotes to real sources, or not including accurate page numbers.

Criteria and Marking:

Submissions will be assessed according to the following criteria: 

1.             Ability to construct a well-reasoned and justified scenario.

2.             Depth and breadth of knowledge and understanding of central issues.

3.             Ability to work collaboratively to produce a well-reasoned strategy to handle all 4 scenarios.

4.             Engagement with credible, relevant, and significant academic and other sources of evidence.

5.             Insight and/or creativity in constructing a point of view or argument and writing in narrative style.

6.             Capacity to produce a coherent and well-written assignment using correct grammar and syntax.

7.             Appropriately referenced, as per APA7 (or other standard referencing system) conventions.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
It's time to take that strategic, long-term, futures thinking you have been doing and turn it into a concrete plan of action!

In the home stretch of the course, students are asked to pull together all that they have learned into two final reports: (a) an individual position paper containing a defensible argument about their problem of inequality and what needs to change and why; and (b) a group-generated policy brief containing an actionable, concrete set of recommendations for a time-poor decision-maker who might be persuaded by (a) but needs to be convinced that it is actionable and plausible before investing more time and resources into it.

This is a three-part assignment involving a combination of group and individual work.

Part A: Position paper (Individual assignment = 20%; 2500 words)

Write a position paper defending your analysis of your chosen problem of inequality and your reasons for advocating changes to policy or practice. A typical structure for a position paper is:

  • thesis statement
  • problem analysis
  • argument(s) for proposed change
  • counterargument(s)
  • defense
  • conclusion


Part B: Policy brief (group assignment = 10%; 1500 words)

As a group, construct a policy brief to advise a decision-maker of the plausibility of your proposal. A typical structure for a policy brief is:

  • 1 page executive summary
  • Problem statement
  • Options
  • Recommendation(s)
  • Implementation plan/schedule


Total word count for both parts (A and B) combined is 4000 words.


Week 12 Feedback Event: Teams present overviews of their policy briefs to other teams, mentors, and lecturing staff in week 12 to stress-test their ideas before finalising results and submission.


Part C: Complete Peer and Self Evaluation form (hurdle requirement)

Complete an updated Peer and Self Evaluation form to capture any adjustments in contributions from team members and append it to your assignment for submission.


Consult the Position Paper and Policy Brief Task Sheet under Blackboard for more information.


Note on AI:

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. Students must declare any use of AI on the coversheet to be attached to the front of their individual scenario submission.

In this assessment you can use resources such as your notes, course materials, and other tools, including AI, while completing the task. The focus is not on memorising information, but on understanding, applying judgement, and demonstrating your own thinking.

Academic Misconduct: Referencing

Fabrication or misrepresentation of sources is considered a breach of academic integrity and may be referred for academic misconduct, regardless of performance in other criteria. This includes but is not limited to: citing non-existent sources, attributing false quotes to real sources, or not including accurate page numbers.


Criteria and Marking:

Submissions will be assessed according to the following criteria: 

1.             Ability to construct a well-reasoned and justified position paper.

2.             Depth and breadth of knowledge and understanding of central issues related to the problem.

3.             Ability to work collaboratively to produce a credible and actionable policy brief.

4.             Engagement with credible, relevant, and significant academic and other sources of evidence.

5.             Insight and/or creativity in constructing an original point of view or argument.

6.             Capacity to produce coherent and well-written policy documents using correct grammar, syntax, and clear and concise language.

7.             Appropriately referenced, as per APA7 (or other standard referencing system) conventions.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Complete and attach the AI coversheet and append the group policy brief to the front of your position paper and your updated Peer and Self Evaluation form at the back of your assignment and submit to 'Position Paper and Policy Brief' Turnitin assignment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction

This seminar provides an overview of the course and gives students an opportunity to form into groups of 4, select their design challenge, and create a Team Charter and Peer and Self Evaluation Form. Research design and advanced search techniques will be covered and students will learn about the limits of AI use if working for an Australian government organisation. Students will refamiliarise themselves with argumentation and argument mapping techniques in preparation for the first individual assessment.


Learning outcomes: L01
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction This seminar provides an overview of the course and gives students an opportunity to form into groups of 4, select their design challenge, and create a Team Charter and Peer and Self Evaluation Form. Research design and advanced search techniques will be covered and students will learn about the limits of AI use if working for an Australian government organisation. Students will refamiliarise themselves with argumentation and argument mapping techniques in preparation for the first individual assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Philosophical perspectives on equality and inequality

PROPOSED TOPICS

This week explores the question of whether we should think of equality as a value in light of philosophical debates about

  • whether differences in opportunities or outcomes due to luck are justifiable
  • whether it is ever reasonable and ethical to forfeit equality for some other value
  • whether the pursuit of equality is compatible with rights and justifies greater state intrusion into private domains
  • what the true purpose of egalitarian movements is.


ACTIVITIES

  1. Socratic circle: students discuss philosophical merits of egalitarianism
  2. Argument mapping philosophical argument for/against equality as something we should strive toward.

Learning outcomes: L01, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Philosophical perspectives on equality and inequality PROPOSED TOPICS This week explores the question of whether we should think of equality as a value in light of philosophical debates about whether differences in opportunities or outcomes due to luck are justifiable whether it is ever reasonable and ethical to forfeit equality for some other value whether the pursuit of equality is compatible with rights and justifies greater state intrusion into private domains what the true purpose of egalitarian movements is. ACTIVITIES Socratic circle: students discuss philosophical merits of egalitarianism Argument mapping philosophical argument for/against equality as something we should strive toward.

learning_activities.topic:
Political perspectives on equality and inequality

PROPOSED TOPICS

The many meanings of ‘equality’ in public policy debates

  • Inequality as a value: analyse the diverse ‘problem’ contexts in which ‘equality’ is invoked as a core value, e.g. job incomes, asset ownership, education, health, housing, citizenship rights, Indigenous rights, etc….
  • Arguments about equality: analyse the political arguments and public perceptions about the interplay/trade-offs between equality and other values


ACTIVITIES

  • Consider a range of researchable topics that could inform the future choice of projects on contemporary issues, including ‘Closing the Gap’.  



Learning outcomes: L01, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Political perspectives on equality and inequality PROPOSED TOPICS The many meanings of ‘equality’ in public policy debates Inequality as a value: analyse the diverse ‘problem’ contexts in which ‘equality’ is invoked as a core value, e.g. job incomes, asset ownership, education, health, housing, citizenship rights, Indigenous rights, etc…. Arguments about equality: analyse the political arguments and public perceptions about the interplay/trade-offs between equality and other values ACTIVITIES Consider a range of researchable topics that could inform the future choice of projects on contemporary issues, including ‘Closing the Gap’.

learning_activities.topic:
Economic perspectives on equality and inequality

PROPOSED TOPICS

  • Inequality: Trends, Measurement, and Public Perception
  • Redistribution: Trade-offs, Constraints, and Policy Space

ACTIVITIES

  1. Evidence-based Policy-making: Empirically grounded diagnosis and quantitative thinking.
  2. Data in Practice: Students review using data in policy development.



Learning outcomes: L01, L05

Data in Practice: Students review using data in policy development.

learning_activities.topic:
Strategic thinking

PROPOSED TOPICS

Strategic thinking, strategic intelligence, and scenario planning

  • Strategic responsiveness, robustness, resilience, and anti-fragility
  • Decision-making under uncertainty
  • Plausibility and future scenarios
  • Causal layered analysis
  • Foresight and strategic intelligence/environment scanning v. forecast
  • Strategic thinking v. strategic planning


ACTIVITIES

  • Students conduct a mock scenario planning activity
  • Students commence scenario planning activity for their design challenge

learning_activities.topic:
Narrative scenario-building

PROPOSED TOPICS

  • The function of narrative techniques (story thinking and telling) in futures thinking--translation rather than invention of matrix; surfacing of secondary consequences
  • Risks of narratives as a futures method -- e.g., false specificity, narrative seduction, dramatic v. mundane outcomes bias, etc.
  • Mitigation strategies


ACTIVITIES

  • Students practice translating scenario planning matrix using creative writing techniques
  • Students choose a narrative form appropriate to their scenario to create an artefact from the future (e.g., day-in-the-life vignette; media release; etc)
  • Students pair up to stress test their narratives for too much creative license and to see whether it is apparent what the driving forces and critical uncertainties are (blind inference test).
learning_activities.topic_description: Narrative scenario-building PROPOSED TOPICS The function of narrative techniques (story thinking and telling) in futures thinking--translation rather than invention of matrix; surfacing of secondary consequences Risks of narratives as a futures method -- e.g., false specificity, narrative seduction, dramatic v. mundane outcomes bias, etc. Mitigation strategies ACTIVITIES Students practice translating scenario planning matrix using creative writing techniques Students choose a narrative form appropriate to their scenario to create an artefact from the future (e.g., day-in-the-life vignette; media release; etc) Students pair up to stress test their narratives for too much creative license and to see whether it is apparent what the driving forces and critical uncertainties are (blind inference test).

Team pitch event

Teams pitch their individual scenarios and group strategies (15 minutes) with 10 minutes for feedback from other lecturing staff and mentors.
